retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Many important epidemiological risk factors for ROP were established in the Early Treatment for Retinopathy of Prematurity (ETROP) study. This randomized, prospective, multicenter trial compared the safety of earlier versus conventionally timed ablation of the peripheral retina. [2] The incidence of any stage of ROP was 68% among infants weighing less than 1251 g. In the year 2010, a global number of 184,700 infants and 14.9 million preterm infants developed any stage ROP. [3] Of those afflicted, 20,000 became blind or severely visually impaired, and 12,300 developed mild to moderate visual impairment (see Image. Epidemiology of Retinopathy of Prematurity [ROP]).

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- History and Physical -- Neonatal Intensive Care Unit Course
retrieved_docs.content: Suboptimal weight gain during early postnatal weeks strongly predicts severe ROP. Predictive models incorporate weight gain trends into risk stratification. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- Epidemiology -- Risk Factors Influencing Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several neonatal and institutional risk factors that influence the epidemiology of ROP are as follows: Gestational age and birth weight: These are the most consistent predictors. Infants born before 28 weeks or weighing <1000 g have the highest risk. Oxygen therapy practices: Inconsistent or excessive oxygen supplementation can significantly increase the incidence, especially in units without oxygen monitoring. Resource setting: In high-resource NICUs, ROP is more often restricted to extremely preterm infants, while in low-resource settings, even infants with birth weights up to 2000 g may develop ROP. NICU infrastructure and staffing: The availability of trained neonatologists, oxygen blenders, and nursing ratios influences outcomes. Screening policies: Countries with national ROP screening guidelines (eg, the United Kingdom,  the United States, Brazil) report lower rates of blindness compared to countries with fragmented or absent screening protocols. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment modalities for ROP have undergone significant evolution over the past 2 decades. Traditionally, laser photocoagulation of the avascular retina has been the standard of care for threshold ROP. However, intravitreal anti–vascular endothelial growth factor (anti–VEGF) agents, particularly bevacizumab and ranibizumab, have gained widespread use, especially for posterior zone I disease and AP-ROP. The Bevacizumab Eliminates the Angiogenic Threat of Retinopathy of Prematurity (BEAT-ROP) and the Ranibizumab Compared With Laser Therapy for the Treatment of Infants Born Prematurely With Retinopathy of Prematurity (RAINBOW) trials demonstrated the efficacy of anti–VEGF therapy in regressing neovascularization and reducing myopia compared with laser treatment. Nevertheless, concerns remain about systemic VEGF suppression, long-term neurodevelopmental safety, and disease recurrence, necessitating prolonged follow-up. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the underlying etiology of retinopathy of prematurity to support early risk recognition and prevention strategies. Assess the severity of retinopathy of prematurity using the International Classification of Retinopathy of Prematurity criteria. Apply evidence-based treatment strategies available for retinopathy of prematurity based on disease stage and zone involvement. Collaborate with an interprofessional healthcare team to implement standardized retinopathy of prematurity screening protocols across care settings. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2523
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature, in turn, is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). وفاة حديث الولادة is identified as a concept within the Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology, indicating that neonatal death is recognized as a specific adverse event in pediatric care. Together, these relationships suggest that infants born preterm with low birth weight may develop short stature, which is linked to an increased risk of neonatal death, a critical adverse outcome captured by pediatric safety terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: Gestation abnormality and pregnancy and puerperal disorders are both linked to the condition ولادة قبل الأوان (preterm birth), indicating that preterm birth is classified as a specific type of gestation abnormality and also falls within the broader category of pregnancy and puerperal disorders. This dual classification underscores that preterm birth represents both an abnormality of the gestational process and a disorder affecting the pregnancy‑postpartum continuum, which is important for clinicians to recognize when assessing risk factors, planning management, and providing comprehensive care to patients experiencing or at risk for early delivery.

retrieved_docs.content: avoiding contraindicated drugs in reflux; and maintaining vigilance for serious pediatric adverse events—including tachycardia, umbilical cord prolapse, and neonatal mortality—especially in the context of preterm low birth weight and its long‑term growth implications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: and extreme prematurity with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). These entities share parentage with unspecified perinatal conditions, which are children of perinatal infections (عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك) and are also part of the SMQ for unspecified perinatal infections. Collectively, the network demonstrates how preterm gestation, fetal growth restriction, respiratory distress, cerebral injury, diabetes mellitus, varicose veins, ulceration, and related amputation procedures interrelate, providing clinicians with a structured framework for identifying high‑risk infants, anticipating metabolic and vascular sequelae, and guiding preventive and therapeutic strategies to improve perinatal and long‑term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) are situated within the broader category of Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation, which is classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}. Within the perinatal‑originating conditions, there is a more specific subgroup of Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). This hierarchical organization indicates that infants born with extreme prematurity and unspecified low birth weight are at risk for a range of perinatal conditions, including distinct respiratory and cardiovascular disorders, highlighting the importance of recognizing the underlying gestational and growth‑related factors when assessing and managing newborn health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature (قصر القامة) is clinically associated with a threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر). This interrelationship indicates that infants born preterm with low birth weight are more likely to develop reduced height, and that reduced height itself may signal an increased risk of threatened premature labor. Recognizing these connections can help clinicians monitor growth patterns and anticipate obstetric complications, thereby improving patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.
